title: Building Inspection Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541350 ($1,864,147,476) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541350 ($3,995,784,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $146,226 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541350); class <5: 6,523 firms, avg revenue/firm $193,991 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,360 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 612 firms, avg revenue/firm $827,992 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $129,581 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 305 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,798,961 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $281,537 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,816,294 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $910,250 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,547,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,902,653 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,508,765 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,835,622 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 40
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.source_fact: ASHI says a panelist uses AI to draft narratives, identify components from inspection photos, and reference building codes; it also describes AI managing scheduling, emails and marketing content.
ai_replaceable_share.derivation: ASHI establishes task examples, not task-time shares or a NAICS-wide workforce distribution. I map those tasks to the supplied SOC patterns while retaining physical inspection, review and accountable judgment. Fractions are judgmental; the parent 5413 wage mix is a material scope mismatch.

citations.title: AI and the Future of Home Inspection: Insights from the Field
citations.figure_quoted: uses AI to help draft narratives, identify components from inspection photos, and even reference building codes
citations.scope: ASHI member-webinar panel examples in home inspection; US association audience, no firm-adoption percentage or occupational time study.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: The supplied civil-engineer role can automate drafting, photo documentation and code lookup, but not site diagnosis or licensed judgment.
The supplied architect role has document and communication exposure, while design accountability and site judgment remain human.
Scheduling, follow-up and status reporting are automatable, but field coordination and relationship work remain.
The supplied drafter role is document-heavy; review, measurements and accountability constrain full replacement.
Operations administration automates, while referral relationships, hiring and quality responsibility persist.
Technical write-up and research automate more readily than physical-system assessment and sign-off.
Managerial reporting automates, but supervision, liability and client escalation remain human.
Narrative drafting, photo labeling and code research automate; the physical walk-through, testing and accountable conclusion do not.
Technical documentation and research automate, but physical verification and professional responsibility remain.
Software-development work is AI-assisted but still requires integration, testing and product ownership.
Residual is a mix of inspection support, administration and specialty technical work; the parent-level mix is unsuitable for this six-digit code.

pi_dist.rationale: Residential inspection is usually a discrete local service rather than an hourly bill; reporting-time savings can initially stay with the operator.
pi_dist.source_fact: No fetched source established a NAICS 541350 firm-level service-fee schedule or observed post-AI price pass-through.
pi_dist.derivation: Estimate based on the one-shot residential inspection model; no source establishes the selected retention fraction.

pi_moat.rationale: Physical attendance, an accountable report and local referral reputation support retention, but local fee comparison and low switching costs limit durable moat.
pi_moat.source_fact: No fetched source quantified customer switching, licensing coverage, referral ownership or AI-era price retention for this NAICS.
pi_moat.derivation: Judgmental midpoint balancing physical-service differentiation against fragmented local competition; no source establishes this value.

t50_years.source_fact: No fetched source measured the share of US NAICS 541350 firms materially using AI in automatable functions.
t50_years.derivation: Specialized report and workflow tools are visible, but solo-operator adoption, liability review and uneven digital maturity imply a multi-year path to 50%. No source establishes six years.

current_adoption_pct.source_fact: No fetched source measured material AI use among US Building Inspection Services firms.
current_adoption_pct.derivation: ASHI documents practitioner examples but no denominator. I do not convert them into a prevalence statistic; 15% is an explicit low-confidence estimate.

historical_analogs.value: ASHI documents a transition from handwritten to digital reports, and its AI discussion documents AI-assisted narrative, photo and code-research tasks. This is an operator-workflow automation analogue, not evidence that inspections are replaced.
historical_analogs.source_fact: ASHI states: Today, most home inspectors use report writing software; its AI article describes use to draft narratives, identify components from inspection photos, and reference building codes.
historical_analogs.derivation: The analogue is limited to report-production technology adoption and task assistance; it does not infer adoption prevalence or product replacement.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Workflow efficiency can range from incremental assistance to substantial reporting-time reduction; no measured time-saving range was found.

citations.title: Report Writing Software: How do you choose?
citations.figure_quoted: Today, most home inspectors use report writing software to create reports
citations.scope: ASHI article describing home-inspector reporting workflow; it is not NAICS firm adoption of AI.

citations.title: AI and the Future of Home Inspection: Insights from the Field
citations.figure_quoted: uses AI to help draft narratives, identify components from inspection photos, and even reference building codes
citations.scope: ASHI member-webinar panel examples; task examples, not a representative adoption survey.

owners_60plus_pct.source_fact: No fetched code-specific owner-age dataset or association demographic was found.
owners_60plus_pct.derivation: Estimate for a mature, solo-owner-heavy trade; no source establishes the 60-plus share.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No fetched source documented a NAICS 541350 succession shortage, so the formula flag is false rather than inferred from owner age.

active_consolidators.source_fact: No fetched source established a count of active repeat acquirers of fee-based US Building Inspection Services firms.
active_consolidators.derivation: Absence-of-evidence estimate. Live searches on 2026-07-20 for building inspection services acquisition, home inspection company acquisition, and home inspection private equity surfaced software, franchise and isolated-listing material rather than a verifiable repeat-acquirer set. Zero is not a sourced fact.

buy_mult.source_fact: No fetched transaction-comparable source established EV/EBITDA multiples for $1-10M EBITDA fee-based building inspection targets after market-rate owner compensation.
buy_mult.derivation: Judgmental entry multiple for a small owner-relationship-dependent service business, expressed as EV/EBITDA after normalizing owner compensation. No source establishes 2.5x.

exit_mult.source_fact: No fetched transaction-comparable source established exit EV/EBITDA multiples for a scaled multi-inspector 541350 platform after market-rate owner compensation.
exit_mult.derivation: Judgmental modest platform premium for transferable regional workflow and referral systems, still below recurring advisory assets; EV/EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation. No source establishes 3.0x.

pricing_structure.value: Inspection-software cost is vendor-priced per report, subscription, annual or one-time purchase; the underlying residential service is treated as a local flat per-transaction fee, with no verified NAICS-wide service-price source.
pricing_structure.source_fact: ASHI's survey found software costs depend on whether the program charges a fee for every report, a monthly subscription fee, an annual fee, or a one-time purchasing fee.
pricing_structure.derivation: The source establishes vendor workflow pricing only. I distinguish it from the operator's transaction service price; the flat-fee characterization is an industry-model judgment, not a sourced NAICS statistic.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Workflow cost can be per-report or subscription; service-price retention is separately represented by pi_dist and pi_moat.

citations.title: Report Writing Software: How do you choose?
citations.figure_quoted: charges a fee for every report, a monthly subscription fee, an annual fee, or a one-time purchasing fee
citations.scope: ASHI survey of 79 inspectors' report-writing software purchases; vendor-cost structure, not the price paid for a home inspection.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Deterministic uplift will reflect only the supplied parent-level wage mix and task fractions. The six-year adoption path is slower because deployment must pass liability review and dispersed small-firm adoption.

terminal_value.justification: AI lowers reporting, research and administration cost, but the paid product still requires physical observation, testing, human review and accountability. Demand remains exposed to transaction volume and waivers.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V and A use task/workflow evidence and explicit adoption estimates only. No PE, M&A, franchise or multiple evidence was used for either input.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Vendor workflow costs can be per-report or subscription, while the operator service is modeled as a transaction fee. That supports pi_dist 0.65 for initial retention but pi_moat 0.45 because local fee shopping and referral competition can pass savings through.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 3.33 to 1.00 under the frozen formula.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: Role-level ranges stated in the judgment rows
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: The supplied engineering-heavy parent mix is not representative of this inspector-dominated code, so V is highly sensitive to the dataset mismatch.
M changes materially with the unobserved transferable-platform premium.

reviewer_note: DATASET_MISMATCH: supplied role_mix is 4-digit 5413 and engineering-dominated; it assigns only 2.05% wage share to Construction and Building Inspectors despite this code's inspector-led residential core. I used every supplied SOC unchanged, added one residual judgment, and did not rebuild wage shares. Heterogeneous boundary: score dominant residential home inspection; commercial PCA/engineering-adjacent work and specialty radon, sewer and energy upsells can have different pricing and exposure.

V.arithmetic: Python finalizer: V_raw = labor_share × ai_replaceable_share = 0.4974 × 0.34014 = 0.169185636; V = 10 × min(1, V_raw / 0.25) = 6.76742544

A.arithmetic: Python finalizer: A = 10 when t50_years = 0, otherwise min(10, 10 / t50_years); t50_years = 6, A = 1.66666666667

M.arithmetic: Python finalizer: M = clamp(4 × (exit_mult − buy_mult) / buy_mult, 0, 10) = 0.8
